food_subgroup: Whipped cream cheese topping / cheese foam

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for whipped sea-salt cheese foam topping. Closest parent 'cream cheese food product' (FOODON_03315695) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cream cheese) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for whipped sea-salt cheese foam as a beverage topping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 'Cream cheese' is the closest parent concept. No specific SNOMED CT code exists for sea salt cheese foam.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sea salt cheese foam is approximately 60 g, representing a single portion of whipped cream cheese topping as served over a beverage or dessert.

nutrient_source.title: Cream Cheese, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170851); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods data for whipping cream (FDC ID 790122) and sea salt mineral contribution
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sea salt cheese foam is a whipped preparation of cream cheese and heavy whipping cream with sea salt. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cream cheese (FDC ID 170851) as the dominant ingredient, blended proportionally with USDA whipping cream data (FDC ID 790122) to reflect the whipped foam composition (approximately 70% cream cheese, 30% whipping cream by weight). Sodium is elevated to reflect added sea salt. No exact match exists in any approved primary regional source for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dairy foods — cream cheese and full-fat dairy analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sea salt cheese foam. GI of 27 is estimated from full-fat dairy products (cream cheese, heavy cream) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Full-fat dairy consistently shows GI in the range 15–35 due to high fat content slowing glucose absorption. GL per serving = (27 × 8.2 × 0.60) / 100 = 1.33, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from added sea salt (~380 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid sea salt cheese foam due to its elevated sodium content (~228 mg per 60 g serving), which contributes meaningfully toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~27) but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sea salt cheese foam has a low glycemic index (~27) and low glycemic load per serving (~1.4), making it unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose spikes; however, its high saturated fat content (17.8 g/100 g) and caloric density (310 kcal/100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance and weight gain if consumed regularly, warranting moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (17.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (92 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea salt cheese foam is high in saturated fat (17.8 g/100 g) and dietary cholesterol (92 mg/100 g), both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this product only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea Salt Cheese Foam (海盐芝士奶盖, Hǎiyán Zhīshì Nǎi Gài) is a popular Chinese-origin whipped dairy topping widely served in Singapore and across East and Southeast Asia. It is made by whipping cream cheese and heavy whipping cream together with sea salt and a small amount of sugar until a thick, airy, mousse-like foam is achieved. The foam is typically spooned or poured over cold beverages such as milk tea, fruit tea, or desserts, creating a visually distinct layered presentation. The flavour profile is simultaneously rich, savoury, and lightly sweet, with the sea salt providing a pleasant contrast to the creamy dairy base. The texture is smooth, thick, and velvety, melting gradually into the beverage beneath it. Nutritionally, sea salt cheese foam is calorie-dense (approximately 310 kcal per 100 g) and high in total fat (28.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (17.8 g/100 g), reflecting its cream cheese and whipping cream base. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg/100 g) due to added sea salt, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. Its glycemic index is low (~27), consistent with full-fat dairy products, so it does not cause rapid blood glucose elevation; however, its high saturated fat and caloric density are relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It contains milk allergens and is not suitable for those with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance. In Singapore, it is commonly associated with Chinese dessert and tea shops and is classified under the dairy and eggs food group.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for sea salt cheese foam exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese and whipping cream entries in proportions consistent with the described preparation. GI is estimated from analogous full-fat dairy data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA SR Legacy cream cheese (FDC ID 170851) and USDA Foundation Foods whipping cream (FDC ID 790122), blended at approximately 70:30 ratio. No exact approved-source match exists for this specific whipped preparation. 2. Sodium is elevated above baseline cream cheese values to reflect the addition of sea salt as a characterising ingredient. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source data for cream cheese and whipping cream; dairy products contain no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; dairy cream products contain no vitamin C. 5. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate from full-fat dairy reference values (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). GL per serving = (27 × 8.2 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 1.4. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (7.4), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1), trans_fat_g (0.5), potassium_mg (138.0), calcium_mg (110.0), iron_mg (0.2), cholesterol_mg (92.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (265.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.02), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.14), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.22), folate_mcg_dfe (9.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.3), vitamin_e_mg (0.7), phosphorus_mg (105.0), magnesium_mg (9.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (3.5), water_g (53.0) are all adapted from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese and whipping cream data. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all fields are estimable from the USDA source data. 7. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; the product may or may not be halal-certified depending on the specific establishment. 8. This product contains milk and is not suitable for those with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance, as noted in the original source description.
